chapterSummary: After the accident, Nick confronts Gatsby, who reveals the full, tragic story of his past with Daisy, his carefully constructed persona, and his enduring, almost delusional, hope. Meanwhile, George Wilson, consumed by grief and suspicion, begins his relentless hunt for the yellow car and its driver, culminating in Gatsby's murder and Wilson's suicide.

characters.name: Nick Carraway
characters.description: Disturbed and sleepless, he tries to warn Gatsby, listens to his full story, and ultimately expresses his profound admiration for Gatsby's incorruptible dream, before being alienated by Jordan and witnessing the tragic climax.

knowledge.content: Gatsby's deep-seated belief that Daisy never truly loved Tom, and his desperate hope that she would come to him after the accident.
The full, detailed story of Gatsby's initial romance with Daisy in Louisville, his deception about his social standing, and his profound commitment to her.
Gatsby's realization that Daisy's world was one of inherited wealth and privilege, which he could only access through deception.
Gatsby's eventual understanding that Daisy, despite her charm, was a product of her artificial world and ultimately chose the security of money over his love.
His final, heartfelt compliment to Gatsby, declaring him 'worth the whole damn bunch put together,' despite his disapproval of Gatsby's methods.
Jordan Baker's continued detachment and her decision to leave Daisy's house, further alienating him.
His complete disinterest in continuing his relationship with Jordan Baker.
Gatsby's murder by George Wilson.
George Wilson's suicide.

characters.name: Jay Gatsby
characters.description: Clinging to a desperate hope for Daisy, he reveals the full, tragic history of his love and the construction of his persona, before being murdered by George Wilson.

knowledge.content: Daisy's failure to come to him after the accident, and her turning out the light at 4 AM.
The detailed account of his first meeting and romance with Daisy in Louisville, his deception about his social status, and his profound commitment to her.
His understanding that Daisy's world was one of inherited wealth and privilege, which he could only enter through a fabricated identity.
His unwavering belief that Daisy never truly loved Tom, and his conviction that she was merely frightened by Tom's accusations.
His realization that he had 'lost the old warm world' and paid a high price for living too long with a single dream, just before his death.

characters.name: George B. Wilson
characters.description: Consumed by grief and a growing, delusional conviction that his wife was murdered by her lover, he relentlessly seeks the owner of the yellow car, ultimately leading him to Gatsby's mansion.

knowledge.content: His discovery of a new, expensive dog-leash, confirming his wife's infidelity and leading to his conviction that she was murdered by her lover.
His belief that the eyes of Doctor T. J. Eckleburg are the eyes of God, seeing everything, and that God will reveal Myrtle's murderer.
His unwavering determination to find the owner of the yellow car that killed Myrtle.
He learns Gatsby's name and location from someone in West Egg.

characters.name: Michaelis
characters.description: The kind and patient neighbor who stays with George Wilson through the night, attempting to comfort him and make sense of his grief and growing paranoia.

knowledge.content: George Wilson's deteriorating mental state, marked by incoherent muttering and a growing obsession with the yellow car.
George Wilson's revelation that Myrtle had been bruised and her nose swollen a couple of months prior, indicating domestic violence or another incident related to her affair.
George Wilson's delusional belief that the eyes of Doctor T. J. Eckleburg are the eyes of God.

characters.name: Jordan Baker
characters.description: Detached and cynical, she calls Nick from Hempstead, further alienating him with her casual indifference to the unfolding tragedy.

knowledge.content: She has left Daisy's house and is moving on to Southampton, indicating her characteristic avoidance of difficult situations.
Nick's coldness towards her after the previous night's events.

characters.name: Daisy Buchanan
characters.description: Remains unseen but is the subject of Gatsby's desperate hope and the catalyst for his tragic end, having retreated into her privileged world.

characters.name: Tom Buchanan
characters.description: Remains unseen but his actions from the previous chapter continue to influence the narrative, particularly George Wilson's vengeful pursuit.

characters.name: Myrtle Wilson
characters.description: Her death continues to drive the plot, as her husband seeks revenge.

characters.name: Catherine
characters.description: Myrtle's sister, found drunk and unable to comprehend the tragedy of Myrtle's death.

knowledge.content: Her inability to understand that Myrtle's body had already been taken to Flushing due to her extreme intoxication.

characters.name: Doctor T. J. Eckleburg
characters.description: The eyes on the billboard, interpreted by George Wilson as the eyes of God.

characters.name: Gatsby's Gardener
characters.description: The last of Gatsby's original servants, who discovers Wilson's body.

knowledge.content: He discovers George Wilson's body near the pool.

characters.name: Gatsby's Butler
characters.description: One of Wolfshiem's new men, who waits for a phone call for Gatsby.

characters.name: Gatsby's Chauffeur
characters.description: One of Wolfshiem's new men, who helps Gatsby with the mattress and hears the shots.

knowledge.content: He hears the gunshots that kill Gatsby and Wilson.

characters.name: Watcher
characters.description: One of the men who stayed with Wilson at the garage, returning in the morning.

locations.name: Gatsby's Mansion
locations.description: Now appearing enormous and musty, filled with dust, reflecting Gatsby's internal state and the cessation of his parties.
lore: Its grandeur now feels empty and neglected, a monument to a lost dream.

locations.name: Drawing-room (Gatsby's Mansion)
locations.description: A room with French windows, where Nick and Gatsby smoke and talk.

locations.name: Louisville
locations.description: Daisy's hometown, the setting of Gatsby's first romance and his subsequent heartbroken return.
lore: For Gatsby, the city is imbued with a melancholy beauty, forever tied to his memories of Daisy.

locations.name: Camp Taylor
locations.description: A military camp where Gatsby was stationed and first met Daisy.

locations.name: Daisy Fay's House (Louisville)
locations.description: The 'beautiful house' where Daisy lived, which Gatsby found excitingly desirable and full of 'ripe mystery'.
lore: Symbolizes the world of inherited wealth and privilege that Gatsby aspired to enter through Daisy.

locations.name: Oxford
locations.description: The university Gatsby briefly attended, where he received Daisy's letter about marrying Tom.

locations.name: Hempstead
locations.description: A town on Long Island where Jordan Baker is staying after leaving Daisy's house.

locations.name: Southampton
locations.description: A wealthy resort town on Long Island, where Jordan Baker plans to go.

locations.name: Flushing
locations.description: The location where Myrtle Wilson's body was taken by ambulance.

locations.name: Office (Wilson's Garage)
locations.description: The small office where George Wilson grieves and obsesses over Myrtle's death, surrounded by the evidence of his tragedy.
lore: The workbench is stained with Myrtle's blood, a constant reminder of her death.

locations.name: Port Roosevelt
locations.description: A location where George Wilson's movements are traced after leaving his garage.

locations.name: Gad's Hill
locations.description: A location where George Wilson bought a sandwich and coffee during his search.

locations.name: Gatsby's Pool
locations.description: The marble swimming pool where Gatsby is murdered, a place he never used all summer.
lore: Becomes the site of Gatsby's death, a tragic end to his summer of longing.

threads.description: Nick's disillusionment with the East and its moral landscape
threads.development: Nick's disillusionment deepens as he witnesses Gatsby's tragic end, the Buchanans' indifference, and Jordan's detachment.

threads.description: The mystery surrounding Jay Gatsby and his true nature
threads.development: Gatsby reveals the full, tragic story of his past, his love for Daisy, and the construction of his persona, resolving the mystery of his origins and motivations, but leading to his death.

threads.description: The crumbling marriage and moral decay of Tom and Daisy Buchanan
threads.development: The Buchanans' moral decay is highlighted by their indifference to the consequences of their actions, leaving Gatsby to take the fall and retreat into their privileged world.

threads.description: The social and moral contrast between East Egg and West Egg
threads.development: The contrast is starkly drawn as Gatsby, representing West Egg's striving, is destroyed by the carelessness of East Egg's old money.

threads.description: The destructive nature of Tom Buchanan's affair with Myrtle Wilson
threads.development: Myrtle's death, a direct consequence of the affair, leads to George Wilson's vengeful pursuit, which converges on Gatsby.

threads.description: Nick's evolving relationship and moral judgment of Jordan Baker
threads.development: Nick's relationship with Jordan is fractured by her continued detachment and his own moral exhaustion, leading to a definitive break.

threads.description: Gatsby's singular romantic obsession with Daisy Buchanan
threads.development: Gatsby's obsession reaches its tragic conclusion as he waits in vain for Daisy, revealing the full depth of his devotion and the futility of his dream, ending with his death.

threads.description: The tragic consequences of reckless behavior and moral irresponsibility
threads.development: The reckless actions of Tom and Daisy directly lead to Myrtle's death and Gatsby's murder, bringing the thread to a critical, violent climax.

threads.description: George Wilson's vengeful pursuit of Myrtle's killer
threads.development: Consumed by grief and a delusional conviction, George Wilson relentlessly tracks down and murders Gatsby, believing him to be his wife's killer, then commits suicide, resolving this thread violently.

scenes.summary: Sleepless and disturbed, Nick rushes to Gatsby's mansion at dawn, finding him dejected and waiting in vain for Daisy. Gatsby recounts Daisy's brief appearance at her window, and Nick urges him to leave town to avoid being traced for the accident. Gatsby, however, refuses, clinging to the last vestiges of his hope for Daisy, prompting him to finally reveal the full, tragic story of his past.

scenes.summary: As dawn breaks, Gatsby, still clinging to the hope of Daisy's call, challenges Nick's belief that Daisy loved Tom, insisting she was merely frightened. Nick, preparing to leave, offers Gatsby his only compliment, declaring him 'worth the whole damn bunch put together.' Gatsby, with his radiant smile, accepts the praise, but his lingering hope for Daisy's call underscores his tragic devotion. The gardener's mention of draining the pool foreshadows an end to the summer's illusions.

scenes.summary: In his New York office, Nick receives a call from Jordan Baker, whose voice now sounds harsh and dry. She informs him she's left Daisy's house and is moving on, then confronts him about his coldness the previous night. Their conversation is strained and abrupt, ending with Nick's realization that he no longer cares to continue their relationship, reflecting his growing disillusionment with the East's superficiality.

scenes.summary: Nick recounts the events at Wilson's garage after Myrtle's death. Michaelis, a kind neighbor, stays with the distraught George Wilson through the night. Wilson, consumed by grief and a growing paranoia, reveals his discovery of Myrtle's affair through a new dog-leash and his wife's bruised face. He becomes convinced that her lover murdered her and that the eyes of Doctor T. J. Eckleburg are the eyes of God, seeing everything. He vows to find the owner of the yellow car, his grief twisting into a vengeful obsession.

scenes.summary: Gatsby, still waiting for Daisy's call, decides to use his pool for the first time all summer, floating on an air mattress. Meanwhile, George Wilson, having tracked Gatsby to West Egg, arrives at the mansion, convinced Gatsby is his wife's killer. The chauffeur hears shots, and Nick, arriving anxiously, joins the staff in rushing to the pool. They discover Gatsby's lifeless body floating in the water, and then Wilson's body nearby, bringing a violent and tragic end to Gatsby's dream and Wilson's vengeful quest.
